title: Logging Platform — System Design
description: Design a centralized logging system: ingest enormous volumes of log events from many services, index recent logs for fast search, retain history cheaply, and alert on patterns. The themes are high-throughput durable ingestion, time-bucketed indexing, hot/cold storage tiering, and the cost-vs-searchability tradeoff.

nodes.label: Application / Agent
nodes.description: Emits log lines; a local agent batches and ships them.

nodes.label: Log Collector (Edge)
nodes.description: Receives, parses, and enriches log batches; accepts fast.

nodes.label: Ingestion Buffer (Kafka)
nodes.description: Durable buffer decoupling ingestion from indexing; absorbs spikes.

nodes.label: Processing / Parse Pipeline
nodes.description: Parses, structures, samples, and routes log events.

nodes.label: Indexer
nodes.description: Builds time-bucketed inverted indexes for search.

nodes.label: Hot Search Index
nodes.description: Recent logs indexed for fast full-text/structured search.

nodes.label: Raw Log Store (Object)
nodes.description: Cheap durable archive of all raw log events.

nodes.label: Cold / Archive Tier
nodes.description: Compressed long-term retention; searchable on demand (rehydrate).

nodes.label: Query Service
nodes.description: Resolves search/aggregation queries across hot index and cold store.

nodes.label: Engineer / Dashboard
nodes.description: Searches logs, builds dashboards, sets alerts.

nodes.label: Alerting Service
nodes.description: Evaluates log-based rules and fires alerts on matches/thresholds.

nodes.label: Retention / Lifecycle Worker
nodes.description: Ages data from hot to cold to deletion per retention policy.

nodes.label: Control Plane / Metadata Store
nodes.description: Tenants, sources, parser schemas, retention policies, alert rules, segment catalog, query jobs, and quotas.

capacityDiagram: Hot[7 days hot + indexed: ~70 TB raw, ~100 TB index]

functional: Ingest log events from thousands of services/hosts across many tenants/teams.
Search recent logs by free text and structured fields, with time range.
Aggregate (counts, group-by) over logs for dashboards.
Retain logs for a configurable period per tenant/service; old logs still retrievable.
Alert when logs match a rule or cross a threshold (rule CRUD + routing).
Isolate tenants/projects: a team only searches and alerts on logs it owns.
Redact secrets/PII at collection and audit every search query.
nonFunctional: Very high ingest throughput (~10 TB/day after sampling, ~2M events/sec peak), bursty.
Durable ingestion: don't drop accepted logs (at-least-once until deduped).
Fast search over recent (hot) data (seconds); slower acceptable for old (cold) data (async).
Cost-efficient retention — most logs are written and never read.
Resilient: a slow consumer or indexer must not drop ingestion.
Secure: authenticated sources, RBAC on search/alerts, encryption at rest/in transit.
Fair: per-tenant quotas so one noisy service can't starve others.

capacity.label: Ingest volume
capacity.note: Assume ~5,000 services, ~20M events/sec is the headline; we size to ~700K events/sec average at ~500 B/event -> ~350 MB/sec -> ~30 TB/day before sampling, ~10 TB/day kept after sampling debug noise.

capacity.label: Events/sec
capacity.note: Burst multiplier ~3x during incidents (the surge that stresses indexers). Drives stream partition count and collector fleet size.

capacity.label: Write:read
capacity.note: Search QPS ~50-200; most logs are written and never read. This skew is why only a hot window is indexed.

capacity.label: Stream partitions
capacity.note: At ~350 MB/sec and ~5-10 MB/sec per partition consumer, ~256 partitions gives parallelism + burst headroom; partition by tenant/service.

capacity.label: Hot window
capacity.note: ~10 TB/day x 7 = ~70 TB raw; inverted index runs ~1-1.5x raw, so ~100 TB hot index. With ~5 TB/shard that is ~20-30 hot shards.

capacity.label: Cold retention
capacity.note: ~10 TB/day compressed ~10x in columnar object storage -> ~1 TB/day stored -> ~365 TB/year. Cheap object storage, searched on demand.

capacity.label: Search latency
capacity.note: Hot index returns in seconds; cold queries become async jobs (rehydrate + scan) bounded by time/service filters and a cost cap.

capacity.label: Indexing lag target
capacity.note: Recent logs searchable within ~30s of ingest under normal load; bounded by scaling consumers off the buffer during bursts.

api.description: Ingest a batch of log events from an authenticated agent. At-least-once: ack only after durable buffer append; retries may resend a batch (deduped by batch_id). Returns 202 on accept, 207 on partial acceptance (some events rejected/quarantined), 429 with Retry-After when the source exceeds its quota or the buffer is saturated (shed before ack, never silently after).
api.request:
Headers: Authorization, X-Tenant-Id, X-Source-Token, Content-Encoding: gzip
{ "batch_id": "uuid", "schema_version": 3, "source": { "service": "api", "env": "prod", "host": "h7" }, "events": [ { "seq": 1001, "ts": ..., "level": "error", "trace_id": "...", "span_id": "...", "msg": "..." } ] }

api.description: Search logs by query + time range, scoped to the caller's tenant (RBAC enforced) and audited. Recent ranges return synchronously from the hot index; ranges beyond the hot window return a query job to poll (see GET /v1/query-jobs/{id}). Supports pagination cursors, result limits, aggregation syntax, and a per-query timeout/cost cap.
Poll an async cold-query job. Large historical queries require time/service filters and run under a cost cap; results (or a temporary rehydrated index) expire after a TTL.
Create or update a streaming alert rule (RBAC-scoped to a tenant). Rule CRUD + list + test live under /v1/alert-rules; the alert service evaluates them on the ingest stream.

dataModel.name: log_event
dataModel.note: A structured log record (parsed from the raw line).

fields.name: message
fields.type: text (full-text indexed in hot; redacted at collection)

dataModel.name: index_segment
dataModel.note: Time-bucketed immutable index segment; rebuildable from buffer/raw.

dataModel.name: retention_policy
dataModel.note: Per-tenant/service retention + tiering rules the lifecycle worker enforces.

dataModel.name: parser_schema
dataModel.note: Versioned parse/field-mapping rules (schema evolution + cardinality limits).

dataModel.name: alert_rule
dataModel.note: A streaming alert rule with ownership and routing.

dataModel.name: alert_window
dataModel.note: Live per-rule window state kept by the alert service (survives restarts).

dataModel.name: alert_event
dataModel.note: A fired alert (for history, audit, and delivery retry).

dataModel.name: query_job
dataModel.note: An async cold-query / rehydration job with cost + TTL bounds.

dataModel.name: tenant_usage_quota
dataModel.note: Per-tenant ingest/index quotas and usage for fairness + 429s.

patterns.name: Durable ingest buffer (Kafka)
patterns.what: Accept logs into a durable partitioned stream that decouples producers from indexers.
patterns.whenToUse: High-throughput ingestion where consumers vary in speed.

patterns.name: Parse & structure pipeline
patterns.what: Turn raw lines into structured events (fields, levels) for indexing and querying.

patterns.name: Time-bucketed indexing
patterns.what: Build immutable index segments per time window so search prunes by time and old segments age out.
patterns.whenToUse: Append-only, time-ordered data dominated by recent queries.

patterns.name: Hot/cold tiering
patterns.what: Keep recent data indexed on fast storage; age old data to cheap cold storage.
patterns.whenToUse: Retention where most reads target recent data.

patterns.name: Sampling / quotas
patterns.what: Drop or downsample low-value high-volume logs to control cost.

patterns.name: Streaming alert rules
patterns.what: Evaluate alert conditions on the ingest stream, not by polling the index.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Write Logs Straight to a Database and Scan It (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Each service writes its log lines straight into a single shared store — one big table or one growing set of files — and when an engineer needs to investigate, they run a query that scans the whole store, effectively grep over everything.

This works for one small service and immediately exposes three problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, synchronous writes can't absorb a ~10 TB/day firehose — a burst or a slow store backs up and stalls the applications doing the logging. Second, searching means scanning the whole store, which gets unusably slow as it grows. Third, it grows without bound; nothing ages old data out, so cost and size explode.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: put a durable buffer in front so ingestion never blocks, build a time-bucketed index so search prunes instead of scans, and tier old data to cheap storage with a retention policy.

view.caption: An app agent ships logs to a collector that writes raw lines to object storage, which the query service scans for engineer searches.

steps.decisionPrompt: If every log line is a synchronous insert and every search is a full-table scan, what breaks first at a TB/day?
whyNow: Establishing the write-and-scan baseline first makes it obvious why ingestion needs a durable buffer, why search needs an index, and why retention needs tiering — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Services write log lines straight to one shared store; engineers scan it to search.
recap.newRisk: Synchronous writes can't absorb the firehose, full scans are too slow, and the store grows unbounded.

traps.trap: Logging directly into a single shared store and grepping it.
traps.why: Ingestion stalls applications under load, search scans everything, and storage grows without bound.
traps.instead: Buffer ingestion durably, index recent logs in time buckets, and tier/expire old data by retention policy.

steps.title: 2. Durable High-Throughput Ingestion
description: Agents on each host batch log lines and ship them to a collector that parses/enriches lightly and appends to a durable, partitioned buffer (Kafka-style). The collector does minimal work so it can absorb TBs/day.
The buffer decouples ingestion from indexing: a slow or down indexer doesn't drop logs — they queue durably and consumers catch up. Partition by tenant/service for parallelism.

Accept fast and acknowledge only after the batch is durably appended; never block the application's logging on downstream processing. This makes ingestion at-least-once: an agent that times out before the ack will retry, so batches carry a batch_id (and events a source seq number) to dedupe duplicates downstream.

Be explicit about overload: if a source exceeds its quota or the buffer approaches saturation, reject or sample before acknowledging (429 + Retry-After) — drop low-value logs at the edge, never silently lose a log you already acknowledged. Malformed or poison records that can't be parsed are routed to a dead-letter/quarantine path rather than failing the whole batch.

view.caption: Host agents batch log lines to the collector, which appends them to a durable partitioned ingestion buffer.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why put a durable buffer between collection and indexing instead of indexing inline?

concepts.definition: A partitioned, replicated stream that buffers accepted logs before processing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples ingest throughput from indexer speed and prevents log loss on consumer lag.
concepts.example: Kafka topic partitioned by service; indexers consume at their own pace.

whyNow: The buffer is the backbone: it's what makes ingestion durable and decoupled, the precondition for everything downstream.

recap.after: Logs durably accepted (at-least-once, deduped by batch_id) and buffered, decoupled from indexing; overload sheds before the ack.
recap.newRisk: Raw lines aren't searchable or structured yet, and volume may exceed what's worth indexing.

flows.name: Ship -> buffer -> ack (never block the app)
flows.note: The collector accepts and durably buffers a batch fast; indexing happens later off the buffer, so a slow indexer never blocks logging.

steps.title: 3. Parse, Structure, and Sample
description: Consumers read the buffer and turn raw lines into structured events: extract level, timestamp, service, and key fields (and a trace id for correlation). Structured fields enable precise queries; the message stays full-text-searchable.

Volume often outpaces value, so control cost here. Be precise about what 'drop' means: dropping from hot indexing (the event still lands in the raw archive, just isn't searchable in the index) is cheap and reversible; dropping before acceptance (never archived) is destructive and should be reserved for clearly low-value classes like healthy debug noise. Enforce per-tenant/service quotas and route different log types to different retention.

Schema evolution is handled here too: parser_schema is versioned, and events carry a schema_version so field mappings can change without breaking old data. Always archive the raw event cheaply (object store) before/while structuring, so nothing is permanently lost even if parsing is lossy.

view.caption: The processing pipeline consumes the buffer, archives raw lines to object storage, and sends structured events to the indexer.

options.name: Tail-based sampling (decide after seeing the trace) (default)
options.description: The processor buffers a request's logs until its outcome is known, then keeps everything for errors/slow requests and drops most healthy debug noise.
pros: Keep all logs for errors/slow requests and drop most healthy debug noise, so you cut volume without losing the logs you actually read.
Decisions use the whole request outcome, so an error's surrounding debug lines are retained.
Targets the real cost driver (high-volume debug logs nobody reads).
cons: Must buffer a request's logs until its outcome is known, adding processor state and latency.
Grouping logs by trace/request id at scale is non-trivial.
A late-arriving log for an already-decided trace can be missed.

view.caption: The processing pipeline consumes the buffer, archives raw lines, and forwards structured events to the indexer after tail-based sampling.

options.name: Head-based / rate sampling
options.description: Each log is kept or dropped at ingest from its own attributes — level, service, a hash — with no buffering, e.g. all errors and one percent of debug.
pros: Decide at ingest from the log itself (level, service, a hash) - stateless and cheap.
No buffering, so it scales trivially to TB/day with minimal processor cost.
Easy to reason about: keep 100% of errors, 1% of debug.
cons: Can't condition on request outcome, so it may drop debug logs that turned out to matter for an error.
Uniform rate sampling distorts counts unless you store the sampling weight.
Picking rates per service is guesswork that drifts over time.

view.caption: A head-sampling processor consumes the buffer and keeps or drops each line by its own attributes before archiving and indexing.

options.name: Keep everything raw, index selectively
options.description: All raw logs are written cheaply to object storage and never dropped; only the inverted index is selective, so anything can be re-processed later.
pros: Never lose a log: write all raw logs cheaply to object storage, only the index is selective.
You can always re-process or grep raw later for a forensic investigation.
Avoids the 'we sampled away the one log we needed' failure mode.
cons: Raw retention cost still grows with the full firehose, even if compressed.
Doesn't reduce ingestion bandwidth, only index size.
Querying un-indexed raw is slow, so the common case still needs the index.

view.caption: The processor archives all raw lines to object storage that ages out to the cold tier, while indexing only a selective subset.

steps.decisionPrompt: Ingest cost is exploding from debug logs nobody reads. What do you do without losing important logs?

concepts.definition: Downsampling or dropping low-value logs and capping per-source volume.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps cost proportional to value when volume is unbounded.
concepts.example: Keep all errors; sample 1% of debug; quota a noisy service.

whyNow: Structuring enables real search and alerting; sampling is the cost lever, which is the headline tradeoff for logging at scale.

recap.newRisk: Searching all events directly is too slow — recent data needs an index.

steps.title: 4. Time-Bucketed Indexing for Search
description: Logs are time-ordered and queries almost always include a time range, so index in immutable time-bucketed segments (e.g. per hour). The indexer builds an inverted index over the message and structured fields per bucket.

Search prunes by time first — only segments overlapping the query range are touched — then matches terms within them. Shard segments by tenant/service within each time bucket so a noisy tenant doesn't bloat everyone's index and queries scope to one tenant. Segments are immutable and independently retire-able, which is what makes tiering clean; each segment tracks a build status and the source offsets it covers, so a crashed indexer can rebuild exactly the buckets it lost.

Guard cardinality: full-text-index the message but only index an allowlist of structured fields (parser_schema.indexed_fields). High-cardinality fields like request_id or user_id explode the inverted index, so keep them queryable via the raw/columnar path rather than the hot index. Expose indexing lag (ingest-to-searchable) as a first-class metric.
Index only the hot window (recent days); older buckets age out of the index even though their raw data persists.

view.caption: The processor feeds structured events to the indexer, which writes time-bucketed inverted-index segments to the hot search index that the query service searches for engineers.

options.name: Time-bucketed inverted index (default)
options.description: The indexer builds an inverted index over message and structured fields per immutable time bucket, so search prunes by time then matches terms within those buckets.
pros: Prune to the last-hour buckets first, then use the inverted index to jump straight to matching 'errors in service X'.
Full-text/keyword search is fast and flexible for ad-hoc log queries.
Per-time-bucket shards drop off cheaply at retention, and old buckets roll to cold tiers.
cons: Inverted indexes are storage-heavy (often larger than the raw logs) and write-amplifying.
Indexing is CPU-intensive, so it's the component that falls behind during a log surge.
High-cardinality fields bloat the index.

view.caption: The indexer builds a time-bucketed inverted index in the hot search index that the query service prunes by time then matches terms.

options.name: Columnar store on object storage
options.description: Parsed logs are stored as compressed columns (e.g. Parquet) partitioned by time and service, so queries scan columns and aggregate rather than hit an inverted index.
pros: Store parsed logs as compressed columns (e.g. Parquet) partitioned by time/service - far cheaper than an inverted index.
Analytical queries (counts, group-bys, aggregations over fields) are very fast.
Decouples storage from compute, so you scale query workers independently.
cons: Free-text 'find this exact error string' is slower than an inverted index - it scans columns.
Needs a structured schema, so messy unparsed logs fit poorly.
Per-record point lookups are weaker than keyword search.

view.caption: The indexer writes compressed columnar files (Parquet/ORC) that the query service scans and aggregates instead of an inverted index.

steps.decisionPrompt: A query is 'errors in service X over the last hour'. How does the index make that fast over TBs?

concepts.definition: Immutable per-time-window index segments over terms and fields.
concepts.whyItMatters: Queries prune by time then search terms; old segments retire without rebuilds.
concepts.example: Search only the 13:00 and 14:00 buckets for a 13:30-14:30 query.

whyNow: Time-bucketed indexing is the core search mechanism and the natural unit for tiering — the structural decision the rest builds on.

recap.after: Fast time-pruned search over recent logs.
recap.newRisk: Indexing and keeping everything hot is expensive — most data is old and rarely read.

steps.title: 5. Hot/Cold Storage Tiering
description: Most logs are written and never read, and reads skew heavily to recent data. Keep a short hot window indexed on fast storage; age older data to cheaper tiers — warm (compressed, slower index) then cold (archive, searchable only on rehydrate).

Make the cold path concrete. Cold data lives in object storage as compressed columnar files (e.g. Parquet) partitioned by tenant/service/date/hour; a catalog maps each time bucket to its objects. A query whose range falls beyond the hot window doesn't run synchronously — it creates a query_job. Large jobs require time/service filters and run under a cost cap (bounded scanned_bytes); the job either scans the columnar files directly or rehydrates a temporary index, and its result (or temp index) expires after a TTL. So the user experience is: recent search returns in seconds; an old search returns a job id to poll, and a too-broad cold query is rejected until narrowed.

The raw store backs both — you can always re-derive an index for an old window if needed. This tiering is the cost story: it keeps the expensive indexed footprint small while retaining everything cheaply.

view.caption: The query service routes recent searches to the hot index and old ones to the cold tier or raw store, while a lifecycle worker ages data out.

options.name: Hot index + cold object store with lifecycle (default)
options.description: A short hot window stays in the fast inverted index while a lifecycle worker ages older data to cheap object storage; queries route by time range across the tiers.
pros: Keep ~7 days in the fast hot index and age older data to cheap object storage, so 1-year retention is affordable.
A lifecycle worker moves/expires data on a schedule, so cost is bounded and automatic.
Last-hour queries hit the hot index; last-month queries fall through to cold.
cons: Cold queries are slow (fetch + scan from object storage) - acceptable but not interactive.
Query routing across tiers adds complexity to the query service.
Rehydrating cold data for a big historical query can be costly.

view.caption: Recent queries hit the hot index while a lifecycle worker tiers older data to cold object storage that the query service rehydrates for old ranges.

options.name: Index everything for the full retention
options.description: The full retention period is kept in the hot inverted index, so every query hits one tier and routing is trivial — no cold path or rehydration.
pros: Every query over the whole year is fast and uniform - no slow cold path.
Simplest query routing: one tier, one index to search.
No rehydration or tier-aware logic for the user.
cons: Indexing a year of TB/day logs is prohibitively expensive in storage and compute.
Most old logs are queried rarely, so you pay constantly for capacity you almost never use.
Directly violates the stated 'can only afford to index 7 days' budget.

view.caption: Every query hits one full-retention hot index, so the query service routes searches to a single tier with no cold path.

steps.decisionPrompt: Retention is 1 year but you can only afford to index 7 days. How do you serve a query for last month?

concepts.definition: Recent data indexed on fast storage; old data compressed on cheap storage, searched on demand.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds the expensive indexed footprint while meeting long retention cheaply.
concepts.example: 7 days hot index; 1 year in object storage, rehydrated for rare old queries.

whyNow: Tiering is the defining cost/searchability tradeoff of a logging platform; routing queries by age is the concrete mechanism.

recap.after: Small hot index + cheap cold retention; queries route by age.
recap.newRisk: Some patterns need detection in real time, not via search; and lifecycle/retention must be enforced.

flows.name: Search routes by time range (hot vs cold)
flows.note: Recent ranges hit the indexed hot store and return in seconds; old ranges rehydrate from cold storage and are slower but acceptable.

steps.title: 6. Streaming Alerts
description: Alerting on logs ('error rate spiking', 'this exception appeared') shouldn't poll the index — that's slow and load-heavy. Evaluate alert rules on the ingest stream as events flow, so detection is near-real-time.
Rules match patterns or compute windowed aggregates (counts/thresholds) over the stream; matches fire alerts. This reuses the same buffer the indexer consumes — alerting is just another consumer.

Behind the stream sits a small control plane: alert_rules (query, window, threshold, owner, notification routing) with CRUD and a test path, alert_window state that must survive restarts so a crash doesn't miss firings, and alert_events for history and delivery retry. Keep window state and dedup/suppression (one page per incident, not per matching log) in the alert service; route to pager/Slack/webhook by rule and handle delivery failures. The index remains for ad-hoc investigation after an alert.

view.caption: The processor streams structured events to the alerting service, which evaluates rules on the live buffer feed and fires alerts to engineers.

options.name: Streaming evaluation on the processor (default)
options.description: Alert rules are evaluated on the live ingest stream as logs flow through the processor, computing windowed aggregates and firing within seconds of a spike.
pros: Evaluate alert rules on the live stream as logs flow through the processor, firing within seconds of an error spike.
Reads off the buffer/stream, so alerting never depends on the (laggy) index being current.
Sliding-window aggregations (error rate over 1 min) are natural in a stream processor.
cons: Rules must be expressible as streaming aggregations; complex historical comparisons are harder.
Stream processor state (windows, counts) must survive restarts to avoid missed alerts.
Backfilling an alert over past data needs a separate path.

view.caption: The alerting service evaluates rules on the live stream from the processor and fires alerts to engineers within seconds.

options.name: Scheduled queries against the index
options.description: Alert rules are saved queries run against the index on a timer (e.g. every minute), reusing the existing search path rather than a stream engine.
pros: Reuse the existing query service - run the alert as a saved query on a timer (every minute).
Rules are just queries, so anything searchable is alertable with no new engine.
Simple to build on top of the search path you already have.
cons: Detection lag is bounded by the timer interval plus indexing lag - often far more than 'seconds'.
During a surge the index falls behind, so the alert that matters most fires latest.
Polling many rules adds repeated query load to the index.

view.caption: A scheduled query runner periodically searches the hot index via the query service and fires alerts to engineers on matches.

steps.decisionPrompt: You need to alert within seconds of an error spike. Do you query the index on a timer?
whyNow: Streaming evaluation gives low-latency alerts and reuses the buffer; recognizing alerting as a stream consumer (not an index poller) is the key insight.

concepts.definition: Evaluating alert conditions on the live ingest stream instead of polling stored data.
concepts.whyItMatters: Near-real-time detection without hammering the search index.
concepts.example: Windowed count of level:error per service > threshold -> alert.

recap.after: Real-time alerts evaluated on the ingest stream.
recap.newRisk: Indexer lag under bursts and retention enforcement must be handled operationally.

flows.name: Streaming alert evaluation (not index polling)
flows.note: The alert service is just another consumer of the ingest stream; it evaluates windowed rules as events flow and fires without touching the search index.

steps.title: 7. Retention, Backpressure, and Reliability
description: A lifecycle worker enforces retention: age hot segments to warm/cold, then delete past the policy — per service/tenant. This is what keeps storage bounded and compliant.

Handle bursts and slow consumers with backpressure: the durable buffer absorbs ingestion spikes while indexers fall behind, then catch up. If truly overwhelmed, shed or sample at the collector before the ack (return 429) — this drops low-value logs at the edge, which is fundamentally different from losing logs you already acknowledged. Per-tenant quotas stop one noisy service from starving others.

Operate it with metrics and drills: buffer saturation / consumer lag, collector rejection (429) rate, processor and indexer lag, dead-letter volume, hot-index health, cold-query cost, lifecycle backlog, and alert-rule evaluation lag.
Because raw events are archived and segments are immutable/rebuildable, an indexer crash or corrupt segment is recovered by reprocessing from the buffer/raw store — no data loss.

view.caption: The lifecycle worker expires old hot segments and ages the raw archive to the cold tier, while the buffer absorbs bursts as the processor catches up.

steps.decisionPrompt: An indexer falls hours behind during an incident's log surge. What happens to ingestion and search?
whyNow: Closing on retention + backpressure + recovery shows the platform stays bounded, fair, and lossless under stress — the reliability/cost requirements.

recap.after: Enforced retention, backpressure-tolerant, rebuildable from buffer/raw, tenant-isolated and access-controlled.
recap.newRisk: Indexer lag means recent logs are briefly unsearchable — bounded by scaling consumers off the buffer.

deepDives.title: Multi-tenancy, security, and privacy (a logging platform is a sensitive data plane)
points: Tenant/project isolation: every event, segment, query, and alert rule carries a tenant_id; search and alert APIs are RBAC-scoped so a team only sees logs it owns.
Authenticated sources: each agent ships with a source token (log_source.auth_token_hash); unauthenticated or unknown sources are rejected at the collector.
Redaction at collection: detect and mask secrets/tokens/PII before the event is buffered or indexed — redaction must happen at the edge, not after the data has already been stored.
Encryption in transit and at rest across buffer, index, raw, and cold tiers; restrict access to production payload logs.
Audit + retention/legal hold: log every search query (who searched what) for audit, and let a legal_hold on a retention_policy suspend deletion.

failureDrills.scenario: An indexer falls far behind during a log surge.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Ingestion keeps succeeding (buffered); recent search lags until indexers catch up; nothing is lost.
failureDrills.mitigation: Durable buffer + autoscale consumers; shed/sample at the collector only if the buffer saturates.

failureDrills.scenario: A hot index node is lost.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Affected time buckets are reindexed from the buffer/raw store.
failureDrills.mitigation: Immutable rebuildable segments + raw archive + per-segment source offsets.

failureDrills.scenario: Malformed / poison log records keep failing the parser.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The bad records are quarantined; the rest of the batch is accepted and the pipeline keeps moving.
failureDrills.mitigation: Dead-letter/quarantine path with alerting on dead-letter volume; never fail a whole batch on one bad event.

failureDrills.scenario: A high-cardinality field (e.g. request_id) explodes the hot index mapping.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The field stops being indexed but stays queryable via raw/columnar; index size stays bounded.
failureDrills.mitigation: Indexed-field allowlist + dynamic mapping limits in parser_schema.

failureDrills.scenario: A user runs an unbounded 365-day cold query.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The query is rejected or capped until narrowed by time/service; cost stays bounded.
failureDrills.mitigation: Async query_jobs with required filters, a scanned-bytes cost cap, and result TTL.

failureDrills.scenario: An incident triggers an alert storm (thousands of matching logs).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: One alert fires per rule/incident, not one per log; on-call is not buried.
failureDrills.mitigation: Windowed thresholds + dedup/suppression window per alert_rule.

failureDrills.scenario: A service logs a secret/token in plaintext.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The value is redacted at collection before it is buffered or indexed; query access is audited.
failureDrills.mitigation: Field redaction + secret/PII detection at the collector; RBAC + audit on search.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Centralized Logging Platform
finalDesign.description: Host agents batch logs to authenticated collectors that append to a durable partitioned buffer. Processors parse/structure events, redact secrets, sample/quota for cost, archive raw to object storage, and feed an indexer that builds immutable time-bucketed inverted-index segments for the hot window. A query service routes searches by time range — hot index for recent (seconds), async cold query jobs for old (rehydrate from compressed columnar object storage under a cost cap). Alert rules evaluate on the ingest stream for near-real-time detection. A lifecycle worker tiers hot->cold->delete per retention. A control-plane/metadata store holds tenants, sources, parser schemas, retention policies, alert rules, the segment catalog, query jobs, and quotas — giving the data plane its tenant isolation, RBAC, and rebuildability. The durable buffer + raw archive + immutable segments (with source offsets) make ingestion lossless and indexes rebuildable under bursts and failures.

view.caption: Agents ship batches to collectors that append to a durable buffer; processors parse, redact, sample, archive raw, and feed the indexer's hot segments, the query service routes searches across hot and cold tiers, alerts evaluate on the stream, a lifecycle worker enforces retention, and a control plane holds tenants, schemas, policies, rules, the segment catalog, and quotas.

functional.how: Streaming rule evaluation on ingest with rule CRUD + routing.
tenant_id on every record; RBAC-scoped search/alerts; authenticated sources via control plane.
Redaction at the collector before buffering; query audit + RBAC.

nonFunctional.how: Small hot index + cheap cold archive + sampling.
Authenticated sources, RBAC, encryption, per-tenant quotas.

tradeoff: Self-hosted agents give full control over parsing, routing, and redaction at the edge but you operate the fleet and its config.
Cloud agents are turnkey and integrate with the provider's logging service, but lock collection to that platform.
Self-hosted Kafka/Pulsar/Redpanda gives high throughput and partition control at the cost of running a stateful cluster.
Managed streams remove operations but cap retention/throughput knobs and add per-GB cost.
makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with built-in scaling removes hand-tuning broker counts and rebalancing.

tradeoff: A real stream engine (Flink) gives stateful windows and exactly-once for alert aggregation but is heavyweight.
Lighter consumers (Kafka Streams, Vector) are simpler but push window/state management onto you.
Inverted-index engines (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) give the richest full-text search but are storage-heavy and operationally demanding.
Label/columnar engines (Loki, ClickHouse) cut index cost and excel at aggregations, but free-text point search is weaker.
Cloud object storage with lifecycle/archive classes is the cheapest durable retention and removes capacity planning.
Self-hosted object storage keeps data in your network but you own durability and tiering.
makesIrrelevant: Object-storage lifecycle rules can implement hot->cold->delete tiering, reducing custom lifecycle-worker logic.

tradeoff: Query-on-object-storage engines (Athena/Trino) let you scan cold Parquet without keeping an index, ideal for rare historical queries.
A warehouse (BigQuery) is faster for analytics but ingesting the full firehose into it is expensive.

say: Confirm massive durable ingest with recent-skewed search and long retention.
Pin write>>>read and cost-efficiency as central.
Establish alerting as a real-time need.
Parse/structure + sample for cost; archive raw.
Time-bucketed indexing of the hot window.
Hot/cold tiering and age-based query routing.
Streaming alerts on the ingest stream.
Tradeoffs: hot window size vs cost, sampling vs completeness, index vs scan.
Mention trace correlation and multi-tenancy as extensions.

expectations: Ingests to a buffer and indexes for search.
May miss tiering, sampling, and streaming alerts.
Reasons about hot-window-vs-cost and query routing quantitatively.
Designs rebuildable segments and burst backpressure.
Discusses multi-tenant fairness and rehydrate-on-demand precisely.

followUps: How do you correlate logs across services (trace ids) at query time?
How do you enforce fair multi-tenant quotas without dropping critical logs?
How would you search cold/archived data without keeping it indexed?
How do you size the hot window against budget and query patterns?
How would you handle a sudden 10x log surge during an outage?
